meta.title: AI Infrastructure Power Index
meta.purpose: Rank countries by their ability to build, power, control, and govern AI infrastructure.
meta.thesis: AI power is no longer only about models. It is about compute, energy, data centres, capital, and sovereignty.
meta.updateNote: Rrankings will be updated quarterly using public datasets.

sourceCategories: IEA energy and electricity demand data
CBRE/JLL/Cushman & Wakefield data centre market reports
cloud region and data centre disclosures

strengths: Largest hyperscale cloud and AI lab base
Large operational and pipeline data centre footprint
weaknesses: High exposure to local permitting constraints
countries.strategicRisk: Power availability and transmission constraints could become the binding limit on new AI clusters.
countries.verdict: The leading AI infrastructure power, with energy delivery now the decisive constraint.

strengths: Very large domestic data centre and cloud market
Large energy system and state-directed investment capacity
weaknesses: Uneven regional power and cooling conditions
countries.strategicRisk: Export controls and chip supply constraints may limit frontier compute density despite scale advantages.
countries.verdict: A scale leader with strong sovereign control but constrained access to frontier chips.

weaknesses: Reliance on imported chips and foreign partners
Heat and water constraints for cooling
countries.strategicRisk: Rapid buildout depends on sustained access to foreign compute supply chains and cloud partners.
countries.verdict: A rising infrastructure power using capital and energy abundance to close the scale gap.

countries.strategicRisk: Physical scarcity may cap future data centre expansion unless efficiency and imported power improve.
countries.verdict: A governance and connectivity hub whose ceiling is set by land and energy scarcity.

strengths: Growing AI lab and cloud ecosystem
weaknesses: Smaller hyperscale footprint than US and China
countries.strategicRisk: France must convert energy advantage into faster infrastructure delivery to maintain European leadership.
countries.verdict: Europe’s strongest energy-sovereignty candidate for AI infrastructure scale-up.

strengths: Strong AI research and lab presence
countries.strategicRisk: Compute ambitions may be slowed by energy price and grid constraints.
countries.verdict: A research leader whose infrastructure position depends on power-sector execution.

strengths: Attractive sites for large energy-intensive facilities
countries.strategicRisk: Canada risks becoming a host market unless sovereign compute and domestic capital deepen.
countries.verdict: A high-potential energy host with room to strengthen sovereign compute control.

weaknesses: Distance from major northern hemisphere AI hubs
countries.strategicRisk: Grid modernization and firming capacity will determine whether renewable potential becomes AI infrastructure advantage.
countries.verdict: A credible regional infrastructure base with energy transition execution risk.

countries.strategicRisk: Energy cost and siting constraints may reduce competitiveness for very large AI clusters.
countries.verdict: Technically strong and reliable, but constrained by cost and physical siting limits.

strengths: Major European data centre and enterprise market
High policy focus on digital sovereignty
countries.strategicRisk: Industrial demand and AI demand may compete for constrained clean power.
countries.verdict: A large sovereignty-minded market held back by energy competitiveness.

weaknesses: Power and land constraints near demand centres
countries.strategicRisk: Domestic semiconductor strength does not automatically translate into sovereign frontier compute access.
countries.verdict: A hardware-adjacent economy with a narrower data centre and energy platform.

strengths: Strategic intent to build AI infrastructure
countries.strategicRisk: The infrastructure thesis depends on execution speed and foreign technology access.
countries.verdict: An energy-capital challenger with early-stage compute and data centre scale.

strengths: Active sovereign AI and compute policy
countries.strategicRisk: Demand growth may outpace reliable power and domestic compute deployment.
countries.verdict: A demand giant still converting market scale into sovereign infrastructure depth.

strengths: Major European connectivity and data centre hub
countries.strategicRisk: Local opposition and grid scarcity could limit future hyperscale expansion.
countries.verdict: A mature hub facing physical and political limits to additional scale.

weaknesses: Limited domestic AI lab and chip ecosystem
countries.strategicRisk: Data centre growth may be curtailed by power-system limits and public-policy pressure.
countries.verdict: A cloud infrastructure hub whose AI expansion is constrained by the grid.
